quizzes.title: W13 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Samuel composition, sources, canonical placement, and textual criticism

questions.question: What do the explanatory asides in Samuel indicate?

options.text: The book has no historical setting
The book is written only for priests
The book addresses later readers who need older terms or customs explained
The book was composed before all events

questions.explanation: Examples such as seer versus prophet and Tamar robe suggest a later audience.

questions.question: Why is 1 Samuel 27:6 important for dating Samuel?

options.text: It refers to kings of Judah, implying a post-division perspective

questions.explanation: The phrase kings of Judah suggests the narrator looks from after the kingdom divided.

questions.question: Why can Samuel not be the sole author of Samuel?

options.text: Samuel dies in 1 Samuel 25 while the narrative continues
The book says David wrote every verse

questions.explanation: The narrative continues long after Samuel death, so he cannot be sole author.

questions.question: Which sources or source-like materials are mentioned?

options.text: Book of Jashar, poetic units, and records associated with Samuel, Nathan, and Gad

questions.explanation: The lecture cites the Book of Jashar, embedded poems, and 1 Chronicles 29:29.

questions.question: How did Samuel-Kings circulate in Hebrew tradition according to the lecture?

options.text: As one continuous story later divided in the Septuagint into Kingdoms
As a single verse in Deuteronomy

questions.explanation: Eusebius and Jerome are noted for the unity of Samuel-Kings in Hebrew, with LXX divisions into Kingdoms.

questions.question: What narrative role does Samuel play canonically?
questions.explanation: Samuel functions as a bridge from judgeship and the no-king problem into royal narratives.

questions.question: Which larger compositional theory is discussed with Samuel?
questions.explanation: Samuel is discussed in relation to Martin Noth Deuteronomistic History proposal.

questions.question: Why is textual criticism especially important for Samuel?

options.text: There are no manuscripts of Samuel
Samuel has prominent textual problems such as 1 Samuel 13:1 and 14:41
Every verse is identical in all traditions

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights difficult MT readings, possible scribal error, and LXX differences.

questions.question: Why is the Septuagint especially important for Samuel?

options.text: It was written by Samuel himself
Its witnesses are earlier and may preserve readings from a different Hebrew tradition

questions.explanation: Earlier Greek witnesses and Qumran evidence make the LXX highly relevant for Samuel.

questions.question: What did Qumran discoveries change about evaluating the LXX?

options.text: They proved the LXX has no Hebrew basis
They removed the need for textual criticism
They dated Samuel to the New Testament
They showed some Greek readings may reflect an alternate Hebrew Vorlage

questions.explanation: Some Dead Sea Scrolls appear to stand behind Greek translations, especially significant for Samuel.

quizzes.title: W13 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Samuel structure, the sha al motif, Hannah song, monarchy, and the Davidic covenant

questions.question: What reading strategy does the lecture use for Samuel?

options.text: Focus on macro-composition and theological purpose

questions.explanation: The course emphasizes Samuel overall structure and framing poems rather than verse-by-verse coverage.

questions.question: What is the proposed macro-structure of Samuel?

options.text: Samuel, Saul, David, and appendix sections

questions.explanation: The lecture outlines 1 Sam 1-12, 1 Sam 13-31, 2 Sam 1-20, and 2 Sam 21-24.

questions.question: What does the Hebrew root sha al mean and connect?

options.text: Ask/request, linking Hannah, Samuel, Saul, and the request for a king

questions.explanation: The repeated ask/request motif runs from Hannah petition to Saul and Israel request for monarchy.

questions.question: Why is Hannah song more than private thanksgiving?

options.text: It is only a legal code
It is written after exile only
It uses expansive reversal language that exceeds Hannah immediate circumstances

questions.explanation: The poem speaks of enemies, death and life, and cosmic reversal beyond Hannah personal story.

questions.question: What does Hannah song mention before any king appears?
questions.explanation: The song climaxes with language about Yahweh king and anointed, signaling the monarchy theme.

questions.question: How is Israel request for a king in 1 Samuel 8 framed?

options.text: Negatively, as wanting to be like the nations and rejecting Yahweh kingship
As a request for no ruler

questions.explanation: The request is treated as a theological rejection of Yahweh rule.

questions.question: What role does Saul serve in the narrative?

options.text: Historical king, foil to David, and embodiment of Benjamin/Judah tensions

questions.explanation: Saul is not merely a historical figure; he also functions literarily and theologically in contrast to David.

questions.question: What is the house reversal in 2 Samuel 7?

options.text: Solomon destroys the temple before building it
David will not build Yahweh a house; Yahweh will build David a dynasty

questions.explanation: Yahweh reverses David plan and promises to build David a house, meaning dynasty.

questions.question: What does the Davidic promise emphasize?

options.text: Seed, throne, kingdom, forever, and father-son relationship

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights seed, forever language, and royal sonship in 2 Samuel 7.

questions.question: How does the broader canon treat the promised Davidic son?

options.text: It says Saul fulfilled it forever
It keeps wrestling with the identity because Solomon is not ultimate fulfillment

questions.explanation: Kings can initially point to Solomon, but his failures prevent final resolution.

quizzes.title: W13 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Samuel closing poems, messianic hope, Davidic covenant tension, and unresolved fulfillment

questions.question: How does the lecture relate 2 Samuel 22 to Hannah song?

options.text: They function as bookends around Samuel narrative
They both come from Chronicles only

questions.explanation: The lecture reads Hannah poem and David closing poem as framing the book.

questions.question: Which motifs link Hannah poem and David psalm?

options.text: Yahweh as rock, divine thunder, death-to-life deliverance, king and anointed

questions.explanation: The shared imagery supports the bookend reading and messianic direction.

questions.question: Why does the placement of 2 Samuel 22 matter?

options.text: It comes after David moral and familial failures
It comes before Hannah is born

questions.explanation: Because David has already failed grievously, the poem complicates a simple David-is-the-final-fulfillment reading.

questions.question: Why is 2 Samuel 22:51 emphasized?

options.text: Its singular king and anointed language may point beyond David to a future seed
It lists the tribes by size

questions.explanation: The singular language raises whether the referent is David or a future messianic figure.

questions.question: What interpretive issue appears in 2 Samuel 23:1?

options.text: Whether the temple exists in Genesis
Whether David speaks concerning the anointed of the God of Jacob

questions.explanation: Translation differences affect whether the verse is read as oriented toward the anointed figure.

questions.question: How does the lecture read 2 Samuel 23:5?

options.text: David claims his house has been perfectly faithful
David admits his house is not so with God yet trusts an everlasting covenant

questions.explanation: The verse is read as candid about David household failure while resting in God ordered covenant.

questions.question: What does the covenant not yet sprouting suggest?

options.text: The promise remains unrealized and awaits fulfillment

questions.explanation: The lecture treats the sprouting language as unfulfilled hope rather than full realization in David house.

questions.question: Why is Uriah appearance among the mighty men significant?

options.text: It removes Bathsheba from the story

questions.explanation: Naming Uriah at the end reminds readers of David adultery and murder.

questions.question: What resonance is noted in David purchase of the threshing floor?
questions.explanation: The lecture links 2 Samuel 24 to Abrahamic purchase language and covenantal themes.

questions.question: What conclusion does the lecture draw about eternal kingship in Samuel?

options.text: It is fully realized in Saul
It is abandoned by the canon
It belongs only to the northern kingdom
It is anticipated but not realized in David or his immediate house

questions.explanation: Samuel ends with covenant hope still awaiting a future anointed king.
